metadata.subject: English Language Arts
metadata.description: Creative Writing 11 deepens students' practice as creative writers across fiction, poetry, creative non-fiction, and script. Students read as writers — studying how published authors make craft decisions — and apply those insights in their own work. The course uses a writer's workshop approach, emphasizing risk-taking, revision, authentic audiences, and the development of a distinctive creative voice. Students are introduced to the idea of writing for real-world publication and performance.
metadata.general_description: An advanced creative writing course using a workshop model to develop students' craft in fiction, poetry, creative non-fiction, and script — emphasizing voice, risk-taking, revision, and writing for authentic audiences.

big_ideas.statement: The exploration of text and story deepens our understanding of diverse, complex ideas about identity, others, and the world.
big_ideas.elaboration: At Grade 11, creative writers engage with increasingly complex ideas about identity, otherness, and justice — and use their craft to explore and express those ideas.

big_ideas.statement: Texts are socially, culturally, geographically, and historically constructed.
big_ideas.elaboration: Creative writers are aware of the cultural and historical context their work inhabits — and make conscious choices about how to position themselves within it.

big_ideas.statement: Language shapes ideas and influences others.
big_ideas.elaboration: The creative writer's primary tool is language, wielded with precision, imagination, and intentionality to create specific effects in the reader.

big_ideas.statement: Creative writers take risks and persevere.
big_ideas.elaboration: The best creative writing pushes into uncomfortable territory — and emerges on the other side, revised and refined through honest feedback and persistence.

big_ideas.statement: Creative writers are observant of the world.
big_ideas.elaboration: The writer's eye sees the world as raw material: a conversation overheard, a light hitting a building, a contradiction in human behaviour — all become possibilities on the page.

big_ideas.statement: Writers write for authentic audiences and real-world purposes.
big_ideas.elaboration: Creative writing comes alive when it reaches real readers — in publications, performances, contests, anthologies, and online platforms. Grade 11 writers begin writing for the world, not just the classroom.

categories.name: Comprehend and Connect (Reading, Listening, Viewing)
categories.raw: Comprehend and Connect — Reading, Listening, Viewing

competencies.raw: Read for enjoyment and personal goals; recognize First Peoples perspectives and diversity; understand land/place influence in texts.
i_can_statements: I can read widely and keep a writer's notebook documenting images, ideas, and observations.
I can engage with First Peoples creative texts as a writer, attending to craft as well as content.
I can analyze how a writer's relationship to land and place shapes their creative choices.

competencies.raw: Access information from diverse sources; recognize how forms and features shape purposes and messages; think critically and creatively; identify bias, contradictions, distortions.
i_can_statements: I can read mentor texts analytically to extract specific, applicable craft lessons.
I can analyze how form, structure, and genre choices affect a creative work's meaning.
I can recognize stereotypes, distortions, or limited perspectives in creative work — including my own.

categories.name: Create and Communicate (Writing, Speaking, Representing)
categories.raw: Create and Communicate — Writing, Speaking, Representing

competencies.raw: Exchange ideas respectfully; respond to texts creatively and critically; demonstrate speaking and listening skills across contexts.
i_can_statements: I can participate actively and constructively in a writer's workshop.
I can give specific, helpful, and kind feedback on a peer's creative work.
I can present my creative work to an audience with confidence and intentionality.

competencies.raw: Use writing processes to create texts for various purposes and audiences; reflect, assess, and refine for clarity and effectiveness; manipulate language purposefully.
i_can_statements: I can work through multiple drafts of a creative piece, making substantive revisions at each stage.
I can manipulate language purposefully — choosing unusual syntax, unexpected images, or surprising structures.
I can explain the craft decisions behind every significant choice in a finished piece.

competencies.raw: Express and support opinions with evidence; use conventions and citations; transform ideas to create original texts across genres.
i_can_statements: I can produce original creative work in at least three different forms.
I can write a craft statement explaining and defending my creative choices.
I can acknowledge all sources and oral traditions that influenced my work.

areas.name: Text Forms and Genres (Reading as a Writer)
areas.raw: Text forms, genres, features, and structures; visual/graphic text elements; narrative structures in First Peoples texts; protocols.
topics: Fiction: short story, flash fiction, micro-fiction, genre fiction
Poetry: lyric, narrative, prose poetry, concrete, erasure, found poetry
Creative non-fiction: personal essay, memoir, literary journalism, lyric essay
Script: stage play, screenplay, radio drama, monologue
First Peoples creative writing: traditional and contemporary
Reading published anthologies, journals, and literary magazines as models

areas.name: Strategies and Processes
areas.raw: Reading strategies; oral language strategies; metacognitive strategies; writing processes.
topics: The writer's notebook: daily observation, image-gathering, question-asking
Generating material: prompts, exercises, automatic writing, imitation
Drafting: following the energy of the piece without over-controlling
Workshop: giving and receiving targeted, specific, helpful feedback
Revision: reconceiving structure, voice, image, and ending
Preparing work for submission: formatting, cover letters, publication research

areas.name: Language Features, Structures, and Conventions
areas.raw: Elements of style; exploration of voice (point of view, humour, irony, satire, wit, perspective); usage and conventions; literary elements and devices; literal and inferential meaning.
topics: Voice: developing a distinctive, consistent, and authentic creative voice
Image: using concrete, specific sensory detail instead of abstract language
Showing vs. telling: dramatizing scenes, emotions, and relationships
Compression and expansion: controlling the pace and density of a piece
Line breaks, white space, and form in poetry
Dialogue: speech that reveals character, subtext, and relationship
Ending: how to close a piece with resonance, not just completion
Irony, humour, and wit as creative modes
